question: Explain why noise in a color image is often more perceptually complex than in a grayscale image. Describe at least two different types of color image noise.
expectedPoints: Color images have multiple channels (e.g., R, G, B), so noise can be independent or correlated per channel, affecting color balance.
Luminance noise affects the brightness, while chrominance noise appears as colored speckles and is often more noticeable.
Common types include Gaussian noise (additive) and impulse noise (e.g., 'salt and pepper'), which can occur in one or all color channels.

question: Compare and contrast the application of a median filter on a grayscale image versus a color image. What is a significant challenge when filtering color images and why does it occur?
expectedPoints: In grayscale, a median filter replaces a pixel with the median value of its neighborhood, effectively reducing impulse noise.
Directly applying a median filter to each RGB channel independently can cause color artifacts because the median operation disrupts the correlation between color channels.
The challenge is to denoise while preserving the relationships between color channels to avoid introducing new, unrealistic colors into the image.

question: Identify one common model for representing color images that is advantageous for noise reduction and briefly explain why.
expectedPoints: The YCbCr color space, which separates luminance (Y) from chrominance (Cb, Cr) components.
It is advantageous because the human visual system is more sensitive to luminance noise than chrominance noise.
This allows for more aggressive noise filtering to be applied to the chrominance channels without a perceptible loss of image quality.
